questions.prompt: Complete: "The museum is free, so you ___ pay anything to enter."
questions.explanation: "It's free" means paying is not necessary, so we use don't have to: you don't have to pay. "mustn't" would forbid paying, and "must / have to" would make paying obligatory — the opposite of free.

questions.prompt: Which sentence gives ADVICE (not an obligation)?

options.text: You must show your passport at the border.
Drivers have to stop at a red light.
You should try the local food while you're here.

questions.explanation: should expresses advice — a good idea, not a rule: You should try the local food. "must" and "have to" (a, b) state obligations, and "mustn't" (c) states a prohibition.

questions.prompt: Complete: "My sister ___ wake up at 5 a.m. because her train leaves early."
questions.explanation: "My sister" is third person singular (= she), so have to becomes has to: My sister has to wake up at 5 a.m. "have to" doesn't agree, and "must to / should to" wrongly add to after the modal.

options.text: You must to finish your dinner.
She has to leave early today.

questions.explanation: The error is (a): must is followed by the base verb with no to — You must finish your dinner. (b), (c) and (d) are all correct (has to + base, should + base, mustn't + base).

questions.prompt: Complete: "You ___ ride a bike without a helmet — it's dangerous."
questions.explanation: "it's dangerous" signals advice against doing it, so we use shouldn't: You shouldn't ride a bike without a helmet. "don't have to" means it's optional, while "have to / must" would make riding without a helmet obligatory.

questions.prompt: Complete the question: "___ I have to book a ticket in advance?"
questions.explanation: have to forms questions with do/does: Do I have to book a ticket? "Must" would not combine with "have to" here, "Have I have to" is wrong, and "Should to" adds a wrong to after should.
